1: Psalm 118 is the culminating psalm of the Egyptian Hallel (Ps 113-118), the collection sung at Passover, Pentecost, Tabernacles, and Dedication (Hanukkah). At Passover, Ps 113-114 were sung before the meal and Ps 115-118 after — making Ps 118 the 'hymn' that Matthew and Mark note was sung at the conclusion of the Last Supper (Matthew 26:30; Mark 14:26). 'Give thanks to the LORD, for he is good; his steadfast love endures forever' — the identical opening as Psalms 107:1 and as the refrain in Ps 136. The formula (hodu la-Yhwh ki tov, ki le'olam chasdo) is the standard liturgical call to thanksgiving used when the ark was brought to Jerusalem (1 Chronicles 16:34) and at the dedication of Solomon's temple (2 Chronicles 7:3).
2: 'Let Israel declare: "His steadfast love endures forever"' — the beginning of an antiphonal structure involving three groups: Israel (the whole people), the house of Aaron (the priests, Psalms 118:3), and all who fear the LORD (a broader category including proselytes and non-Israelite worshipers, Psalms 118:4). This liturgical call-and-response pattern mirrors the structure of Psalms 115:9-11 and Psalms 135:19-20, suggesting a formal temple setting where different groups of worshipers respond in turn. The repetition of the ki le'olam chasdo refrain (his steadfast love endures forever) functions as the congregation's liturgical affirmation.
3: 'Let the house of Aaron declare: "His steadfast love endures forever"' — the priestly house of Aaron as a distinct liturgical body, separate from the laity ('Israel' in Psalms 118:2). The division mirrors the Levitical organization of worship in Chronicles (1 Chr 23-26), where Levites and Aaronic priests served distinct functions. In the Second Temple period, the Levitical choirs would sing the Hallel while the priests conducted the sacrificial rites. The priest's own declaration of YHWH's hesed grounds their mediatorial function in covenant relationship rather than ritual technique.
4: 'Let all who fear the LORD declare: "His steadfast love endures forever"' — 'all who fear the LORD' (yir'ei Yhwh) is broader than ethnic Israel and the Aaronic priests; it is the category that includes Gentile God-fearers who attached themselves to Israel's worship. The same phrase appears in Psalms 115:11 and Psalms 135:20. In Acts, the 'God-fearers' (phoboumenoi ton theon) are Gentiles who participated in synagogue worship without full conversion — Cornelius (Acts 10:2), the Antioch audience (Acts 13:16, 26). The psalm's opening invites this broadest possible congregation to affirm YHWH's eternal hesed.
5: 'From the depths of my distress I called to the LORD; the LORD answered me and gave me room to breathe' — the spatial theology of distress and rescue: min-hammetsar (from the narrow place) is contrasted with the LORD's response in merchav (a wide/spacious place). Distress = narrowness, constriction; salvation = wide open space. The root tsar/metsar is also the root of mitsrayim (Egypt), the paradigmatic 'narrow place' from which YHWH rescued Israel. Personal deliverance is narrated using Exodus spatial vocabulary: YHWH pulls the psalmist from the narrow place of oppression into the wide place of liberation.
6: 'The LORD is on my side; I will not be afraid. What can any person do to me?' — the rhetorical question that expects the answer 'nothing.' The structure echoes Psalms 27:1 ('The LORD is my light and my salvation — whom shall I fear?'). This verse is cited in Hebrews 13:6: 'So we say with confidence: "The Lord is my helper; I will not be afraid. What can mere mortals do to me?"' — applied to Christian confidence in the face of economic threat (the context is freeing believers from love of money and fear of losing it). The theological logic is identical: the LORD's presence makes human hostility ultimately impotent.
7: 'The LORD stands on my side as my helper; I will see my enemies brought to shame' — 'I will see my enemies brought to shame' (ani er'eh beson'ay, I will look upon those who hate me) — the vindication theology of the psalms. In the ancient honor-shame world, seeing one's enemies put to shame was the mirror image of one's own honor being restored. The enemies who attacked the psalmist and sought to dishonor him will themselves be dishonored, while the psalmist lives to witness it (Psalms 118:17: 'I will not die — I will live'). The reversal of shame and honor is YHWH's work, not the psalmist's own vengeance.
8: 'It is better to take shelter in the LORD than to put your confidence in any person' — the first of two comparative trust sayings (tov...min, it is better than). The comparison is not that human help is evil but that YHWH's shelter is categorically superior. The verb chasah (take shelter/take refuge) is the covenant-loyalty term for fleeing to YHWH as protector — the same verb as Psalms 2:12 ('Blessed are all who take refuge in him'), Psalms 5:11, Psalms 7:1. The trust comparison also appears in Psalms 146:3-5: 'Do not put your trust in princes ... blessed is the one whose help is the God of Jacob.'
9: 'It is better to take shelter in the LORD than to put your confidence in rulers' — the second trust comparison specifically targets political leadership: nadivim (nobles, rulers, princes). The distrust of noble patronage was a significant counter-cultural claim in the ancient Near Eastern patron-client world, where powerful patrons were the primary source of protection, justice, and advancement. The psalms consistently relocate the ultimate source of security from the human patron network to YHWH. Psalms 146:3: 'Do not put your trust in princes, in human beings, who cannot save.' YHWH as the superior patron who does not die (Psalms 146:4) and whose purposes endure forever (Psalms 146:6).
10: 'All the nations closed in around me, but in the name of the LORD I routed them' — the first of three parallel verses (Psalms 118:10-12) each structured as: (threat) + 'but in the name of the LORD I cut them off.' The triple structure is liturgical intensification: increasing the sense of overwhelming threat (all nations → surrounded → swarmed like bees) against which the single response is constant and sufficient: the name of YHWH. 'All the nations' (kol-goyim) is the universal scope of the threat, whether literal (a coalition of enemies) or hyperbolic (for emphasis). The 'name of the LORD' (beshem Yhwh) as the instrument of victory: the name represents the divine person and authority.
11: 'They surrounded me — closed in from every side — but in the name of the LORD I cut them off' — the encirclement imagery (sabuni gam sabuni, they surrounded me, yes they surrounded me) is doubled for emphasis — the completeness of the encirclement is total. The image is military: the enemy has closed off all escape routes. Against this complete human hopelessness, the single response remains unchanged: 'in the name of the LORD I cut them off' (ki beshem Yhwh ki amilan, for in the name of the LORD I circumcised/cut them down). The verb mil (to circumcise, to cut down) may carry a double meaning: to cut them off as in military defeat, with the covenant sign of circumcision implied.
12: 'They swarmed around me like bees, but blazed out as quickly as a brush fire; in the name of the LORD I cut them off' — the bee simile (kidvorah daaru) appears in Deuteronomy 1:44 (the Amorites 'chased you like a swarm of bees'), making this a Deuteronomic military image. Bees represent the sudden, multiple, painful attack of a swarming enemy. The antithesis is the brush-fire metaphor: the enemy's threat is intense but brief — it burns like thorn-fire (es qotsim, fire of thorns), which blazes suddenly but consumes quickly (Ecclesiastes 7:6: 'like the crackling of thorns under the pot, so is the laughter of fools'). Their ferocity is real but transient; YHWH's name remains.
13: 'I was shoved to the point of falling, but the LORD was there to help me' — the passive 'I was shoved' (dachoh dechitani, pushed-push you pushed me) acknowledges the overwhelming force of the attack — the psalmist was nearly knocked down. But YHWH was the decisive help (azarani, he helped me). The passive verb leaves the attacker implicit (the nations of Psalms 118:10-12 are the ones shoving). The near-fall and divine rescue is the experiential pattern that grounds the trust sayings of Psalms 118:8-9: having been nearly defeated and rescued, the psalmist knows empirically that YHWH's help exceeds human alliance.
14: 'The LORD is my strength and my song; he has become my salvation' — this is a verbatim citation of Exodus 15:2, the Song of the Sea sung by Moses and Israel after crossing the Red Sea: 'The LORD is my strength and my song; he has become my salvation.' By using this specific language, the psalmist explicitly identifies his personal military deliverance (Psalms 118:10-13) with the paradigmatic Exodus. Personal rescue = Exodus replay. The Song of the Sea is the founding narrative of YHWH's identity as warrior-redeemer; the psalmist claims that identity for his own experience. Isaiah 12:2 also cites this verse, applying it to the eschatological new exodus.
15: 'Shouts of joy and victory echo in the tents of the righteous: "The right hand of the LORD does mighty things!"' — the 'tents of the righteous' (aholei tsadiqim) is a military idiom: the camp of YHWH's warriors (cf. 1 Samuel 4:10; Psalms 13:2; 17:53 where 'tent' = military bivouac). The shouts are those of victorious soldiers returning to camp after battle. The specific acclamation — 'The right hand of the LORD does mighty things' (yamin Yhwh osah chayil) — echoes Exodus 15:6: 'Your right hand, LORD, was majestic in power; your right hand, LORD, shattered the enemy.' The right hand (yamin) is the instrument of divine power in battle; Psalms 118:15-16 repeat the acclamation three times.
16: 'The right hand of the LORD is raised high; the right hand of the LORD does mighty things!' — the raised right hand (yamin Yhwh romemah) is the gesture of victory: a warrior raising his weapon arm after defeating the enemy. In ANE iconography, the triumphant deity or king raises his right hand holding a weapon over a defeated foe. This is YHWH's posture of triumph: his right hand raised, having done mighty things (asah chayil, done valor/strength). The three-fold acclamation in Psalms 118:15-16 creates a liturgical crescendo — building the celebratory energy of the victory processional before the turn toward the temple gates (Psalms 118:19).
17: 'I will not die — I will live, and I will tell of what the LORD has done' — the radical declaration of survival that grounds the psalm's entire testimony. The chiastic structure: not-die / live / tell — the purpose of continued life is proclamation. In Israelite theology, the dead do not praise YHWH (Psalms 6:5: 'No one remembers you when he is dead; who praises you from the grave?'; Psalms 88:10-12; Isaiah 38:18-19). Continued life is the precondition of praise and witness. Hezekiah's prayer in Isaiah 38:18-20 parallels this theology: 'The grave cannot praise you ... the living, the living — they praise you, as I am doing today.' The purpose of survival is testimony.
18: 'The LORD has disciplined me hard, but he has not let death have me' — the yissar/musar (discipline) vocabulary is the covenantal discipline of a father to a son (Deuteronomy 8:5: 'Know then in your heart that as a man disciplines his son, so the LORD your God disciplines you'; Proverbs 3:11-12: 'Do not despise the LORD's discipline ... because the LORD disciplines those he loves, as a father the son he delights in'). Hebrews 12:5-11 cites Proverbs 3:11-12 to reframe suffering as divine paternal discipline. The suffering narrated in the psalm is not divine abandonment but covenant discipline — intense (yasser yisserani, he disciplined me severely) yet bounded: 'he has not let death have me.'
19: 'Open the righteous gates for me so I can go in and give thanks to the LORD' — the entrance liturgy begins. The 'righteous gates' (sha'arei tsedeq) are the temple gates — likely the Eastern Gate through which worshipers entered. The request 'open to me the gates' marks the transition from the outer court to the inner sanctuary. The closest parallel is Psalms 24:7-10: 'Lift up your heads, O gates! ... Who is this King of Glory? The LORD, strong and mighty...' — the other great entrance liturgy of the Psalter. The purpose stated: 'so I can go in and give thanks to the LORD' — the todah (thanksgiving) offering was the specific sacrifice associated with this entrance after deliverance from mortal danger.
20: 'This is the LORD's own gate; only the righteous may enter through it' — the gatekeeper's response to Psalms 118:19's request: the gate is identified as YHWH's exclusive property (zeh-hashsha'ar la-Yhwh, this is the gate belonging to YHWH) and the condition for entry is stated: only the righteous (tsadiqim) may enter. This reflects the 'entrance Torah' tradition: lists of moral conditions for entering YHWH's temple (Ps 15: 'LORD, who may dwell in your sacred tent? ... The one whose walk is blameless'; Psalms 24:3-4: 'Who may ascend the mountain of the LORD? ... The one who has clean hands and a pure heart'). The gate-condition theology presupposes that temple access is morally conditioned, not automatic.
21: 'I praise you because you answered me and have become my salvation' — the thanksgiving declaration upon entering the gate. The todah formula: 'you answered me' (anitani) connects to Psalms 118:5 where the psalmist 'called' (qarati) and YHWH 'answered' (anani). The psalm's narrative arc: distress → call → answer → entrance → thanksgiving — is the classic todah pattern. In Jonah 2, from the fish's belly, Jonah vows the todah: 'What I have vowed I will make good; salvation comes from the LORD' (Jonah 2:9). The todah prayer and sacrifice were the formal celebration of survival after mortal danger.
22: 'The stone that the builders threw aside has become the cornerstone' — the most cited verse of Psalm 118 in the NT, and one of the most significant OT quotations in early Christian theology. Even (stone) rejected by bonim (builders) has become rosh pinnah (head of the corner, cornerstone or capstone). In its original context, the rejected stone likely refers to Israel despised by the nations (Psalms 118:10-12) or the Davidic king dismissed by human powers, now made central by YHWH's reversal. The wordplay between even (stone) and ben (son) in Hebrew may be intentional. NT citations: Matthew 21:42 (Jesus applies it to himself after the parable of the tenants); Mark 12:10-11; Luke 20:17; Acts 4:11 (Peter: 'Jesus is the stone you builders rejected'); 1 Peter 2:7; Ephesians 2:20.
23: 'This is what the LORD has done; it is wonderful in our sight' — the exclamatory response to the cornerstone reversal (Psalms 118:22). 'Wonderful' (niflayt, from pala) is the vocabulary of divine signs and wonders — the term for YHWH's acts that exceed human expectation and capacity (Exodus 3:20: 'I will stretch out my hand and strike Egypt with all the wonders that I will perform'; Psalms 77:11: 'I will remember the deeds of the LORD; yes, I will remember your miracles of long ago'). The reversal of the rejected stone becoming the cornerstone is classified as a miraculous divine act, not merely a fortunate turn of events. In Matthew 21:42-43, Jesus uses this verse immediately after quoting Psalms 118:22.
24: 'This is the day the LORD has made; let us celebrate and be glad in it' — 'the day' (hayyom hazeh) the LORD has made (asah, constructed/purposed) is the day of the reversal narrated in Psalms 118:22-23. In its liturgical context, it is the day of salvation, the day YHWH has brought the rejected one through death's door to celebration at the temple. In Christian liturgy, this verse became central to Easter celebration and then to Sunday worship (dies Domini, the Lord's Day) — the day of resurrection as the ultimate divine reversal of death. 'Let us celebrate' (nagilah, we will exult) and 'be glad' (nismechah, we will rejoice) — two verbs of full-body joy in the divine presence.
25: 'LORD, save us, we pray! LORD, grant us success, we pray!' — hoshi'a-na (save now!) is the word that becomes 'Hosanna' in the NT. The Hebrew phrase ana YHWH hoshi'ah na (O LORD, save now!) + ana YHWH hatslichah na (O LORD, grant success now!) are petitions for YHWH's continued salvific action. At the Palm Sunday entry of Jesus into Jerusalem (Matthew 21:9; Mark 11:9-10; John 12:13), the crowds shouted 'Hosanna!' while waving palm branches and laying garments on the road — the full Hallel processional reenacted with Jesus as its fulfillment. The word shifted from petition ('save, please!') to acclamation ('Hosanna!' as praise), which was already happening in Second Temple liturgy.
26: 'May God bless the one who comes in the name of the LORD! We bless you from the LORD's house' — baruch ha-ba beshem Yhwh (blessed is the one who comes in the name of the LORD) is the priestly blessing pronounced on arriving pilgrims from within the temple precincts ('we bless you from the LORD's house'). In all four Gospels, this verse is applied to Jesus at the Palm Sunday entry: Matthew 21:9; Mark 11:9; Luke 19:38 ('Blessed is the king who comes in the name of the Lord!'); John 12:13. Jesus himself quotes Psalms 118:26 in his lament over Jerusalem: 'You will not see me again until you say, "Blessed is he who comes in the name of the Lord"' (Matthew 23:39; Luke 13:35) — implying a future eschatological welcome that Jerusalem has not yet offered.
27: 'The LORD is God, and his light has shone on us. Bind the sacrifice with cords and bring it all the way to the horns of the altar' — 'his light has shone on us' (vaya'er lanu, he has given us light) recalls the Aaronic blessing: 'The LORD make his face shine upon you' (Numbers 6:25). The light of the divine presence at the temple is experienced by the worshiping community as a gift. The practical instruction follows: 'Bind the sacrifice with cords to the horns of the altar' — the todah (thanksgiving) offering. The horns (qarnot) of the altar were the projections at the four corners where sacrificial blood was applied (Exodus 29:12; Leviticus 4:7) and where the sacrifice was tied before slaughter. The living animal was bound at the altar before the final act.
28: 'You are my God, and I will give you thanks; you are my God, and I will praise you' — the personal declaration completing the psalm's movement from corporate liturgy (Psalms 118:1-4: 'let Israel declare') to individual testimony (Psalms 118:5-18) to the temple entrance (Psalms 118:19-27) to personal address (Psalms 118:28: 'you are my God'). The double 'you are my God' (eli atah; elimated elohay) is the covenant formula — the personal ownership of relationship. The verb odecha (I will give you thanks) is the todah root: the psalm itself is the verbal todah, the declaration of thanksgiving that was inseparable from the sacrifice. The psalm began with 'Give thanks to the LORD' and ends with 'I will give you thanks.'
29: 'Give thanks to the LORD, for he is good; his steadfast love endures forever' — the exact repetition of Psalms 118:1 forms the literary inclusio that closes both the psalm and the Egyptian Hallel (Ps 113-118). The closing formula places the entire psalm — the distress, the deliverance, the entrance, the cornerstone reversal, the Hosanna — within the frame of YHWH's enduring goodness and hesed. The Hallel ended with this verse is the last thing Jesus and the disciples sang before leaving for Gethsemane (Matthew 26:30; Mark 14:26). The psalm that narrates death-and-survival, the cornerstone reversal, and the Hosanna acclamation was sung by Jesus knowing what was immediately ahead.
